Dont' show me your drafts but let's talk strategy for the followups for the people that didn't respond. Last Prisoner Project Overview.  The Last Prisoner Project (LPP) stands as one of the most meaningful and high-impact initiatives in the modern cannabis space. It bridges two worlds — the booming legal cannabis industry and the tens of thousands of people still suffering from outdated, unjust drug laws. Supporting this project isn’t just about charity; it’s about fairness, humanity, and integrity. It’s a chance for the cannabis community to live out the values it often speaks about: justice, freedom, and opportunity for all. Founded in 2019, the Last Prisoner Project was built on a simple, powerful idea: no one should be in prison for an act that is now legal. While legal cannabis is creating billions in revenue, too many people — often from communities most harmed by prohibition — remain incarcerated or burdened with criminal records that block access to jobs, housing, and basic dignity. LPP is dedicated to repairing that damage through legal intervention, policy change, and reentry support for those directly affected by the War on Drugs. The organization’s track record is remarkable. Working with a network of more than 200 pro bono attorneys, LPP has secured the release or sentence reduction of over 300 people, eliminating more than 230 years of unjust incarceration. Its policy team has helped shape legislation across 31 states, contributing to the automatic expungement of over 1.6 million cannabis convictions. When Maryland Governor Wes Moore pardoned 175,000 cannabis records in 2024, LPP’s team was part of the driving force behind that reform. But their work doesn’t stop at freedom — it extends to rebuilding lives. Through reentry programs and direct aid, LPP has distributed over $3.7 million to help formerly incarcerated individuals and their families pay for essentials like food, housing, transportation, and healthcare. They also operate a commissary and reentry grant fund, providing support to those still behind bars or newly released, giving them the chance to truly start over. The organization is led by Stephanie Shepard, who brings both lived experience and leadership to the mission. In 2010, she was sentenced to 10 years in federal prison for a non-violent cannabis offense. After serving nine years, she dedicated her life to ensuring no one else endures what she did. Her story is a reminder that behind every statistic is a human being — a person whose life, family, and future were disrupted by outdated laws. For dispensaries, cannabis brands, and retailers, partnering with the Last Prisoner Project is a way to connect purpose with business. Selling co-branded hemp wick, apparel, or other products with LPP’s logo turns ordinary transactions into acts of justice. Every sale directly supports their legal and reentry programs, helping free prisoners, reunite families, and restore lives. This kind of collaboration also carries real meaning for customers. Cannabis consumers are increasingly value-driven — they want to know that their dollars contribute to something bigger. When they see a brand aligned with LPP, they see authenticity, integrity, and awareness. It signals that the company understands the industry’s roots and is committed to equity, not just profit. It’s a powerful form of storytelling: • Each product sold helps fund the fight to end cannabis incarceration. • Each dollar raised helps bring someone home. • Each partnership builds trust between the legal market and the communities it grew from. Supporting LPP also gives dispensaries and brands a way to differentiate themselves in a crowded marketplace. It transforms their business into a platform for change — one that customers respect, remember, and rally behind. Beyond optics, it’s about real alignment: the people profiting from the new era of legalization helping those who were punished under the old one. In short, here’s what makes this project worth supporting: • It’s morally right. LPP is addressing the fundamental injustice at the heart of cannabis legalization. • It’s effective. Their policy and legal teams are freeing people, clearing records, and changing laws at scale. • It’s personal. Many of their leaders and advocates are directly impacted by the issue they’re fighting to fix. • It’s good business. Aligning with LPP strengthens brand reputation, customer trust, and long-term loyalty. The cannabis industry was built on resilience, community, and the pursuit of freedom. Supporting the Last Prisoner Project keeps that spirit alive. By carrying co-branded products, donating a portion of sales, or simply sharing their message, dispensaries and brands can help transform this industry into a true movement for justice. Because until every cannabis prisoner is free, legalization is only half complete. 11/12/2025, 11:40:00 PM Absolutely. Let’s drill down on strategy for non-responders—especially given the deep LPP mission alignment. First, some clarifying questions for calibration: 1. AUDIENCE NUANCE: Are most non-responders single-location dispensary owners, multi-state operators, brand reps, or buyer contacts? Do any have a public history of advocacy or social impact campaigns? 2. VALUE PRIORITY: Do we want the next touchpoints weighted more toward the emotional “why” (LPP’s impact and story), toward the practical business win (differentiation, customer loyalty), or a blend? 3. PRODUCT FOCUS: Is co-branded LPP wick the only offer in this wave, or can we reference future expansion into other LPP items, like apparel or events? 4. BEST ANGLE SO FAR: Any prior themes or details that seemed to trigger replies, even if not sales? Now, if you want my recommended arc for the follow-ups—in line with your goal to sell wick and create real LPP impact—here’s how I’d shape the outreach logic: --- ### RE-ENGAGEMENT FOLLOW-UP SEQUENCE (Non-Responders) **Day 3: “Proof of Alignment” Bump** - NEW ANGLE: Visual hook—offer a quick mockup of their brand or location co-branded with LPP/NMU wick. - PURPOSE: Signal this isn’t a blast, and remind them of the custom, on-mission approach. - CTA: “Want to see a visual? It’s quick—just say the word.” **Day 7: “Deeper Reason” Bump** - NEW ANGLE: Personalize the mission—emphasize the human side of LPP with a real story or operational proof (e.g., “Every sale helps free or support someone like Stephanie Shepard”). - PURPOSE: Make the stakes real, connect the dots from retail action to justice impact. - CTA: “Does purposeful merchandising matter to your team right now?” **Day 15: “Timing/Occasion” Bump** - NEW ANGLE: Tie in a calendar hook (i.e., “Green Wednesday is coming,” or “A new store drop is a perfect launch moment for something meaningful”). - PURPOSE: Show you know their market rhythm, and align LPP/NMU collaboration with real event momentum. - CTA: “Should we time a launch for your next event or drop?” **Day 31: “Exit Ramp & Reflection” Bump** - NEW ANGLE: Respectful close—the door remains open, invite honest feedback or the right contact. - PURPOSE: Maintain integrity, preserve future goodwill, and gently prompt a final action. - CTA: “Still interested, or is there someone better on your team for this?” **Monthly (if appropriate): “Value Pulse”** - NEW ANGLE: Drop a relevant trend, industry data point, or recent LPP milestone as a gentle check-in. Always offer a new conversation angle, never just “checking in.” --- ### GENERAL GUIDANCE - **Escalation** stays soft (never pushy); each email introduces a fresh hook, not just “bumping up.” - Every touchpoint should remind the reader—concisely—how carrying the wick actually transforms retail transactions into real-world change (both for customers and those impacted by past laws). - Use the LPP story as a live thread: bring forward new facets of their mission in each email, so it’s not repetitive. - Always close with a non-demanding, human question—avoiding any pressure or “last chance” noise. - Reference relevant local events, brand initiatives, or store campaign moments whenever possible for added resonance. --- Let me know: - More about your audience/top targets, - Which value angle is highest priority for you, - And if you want help drafting or refining each bump’s “hook”/CTA for an actual campaign run. 11/12/2025, 11:40:00 PM There are a whole mixed back of dispensaries types from MSO's to mom and pops. I think that this white labeled hemp wick will give value to their clients and also build the incomes for the LPP. WE co branded with LPP and RAW. THey can see it on this website. https://www.lastprisonerproject.org/raw-take-action WE can put that in every email and every follow up. 11/12/2025, 11:46:21 PM Thanks, that context is key. Let’s break out your optimal non-responder follow-up sequence—tuned for the wide mix (from MSOs to independents) and anchored around: • Elevating client value via the hemp wick • Demonstrating LPP income/impact • Leveraging social proof via the RAW/LPP partnership link (subtly, never hard-sell) ### Follow-Up Structure **1. Day 3: Visual/Customization Hook** - Subject: “Quick visual – LPP + your brand?” - Angle: Offer to mock up co-branded packaging so they see themselves in the movement. - CTA: “Want to see your logo in action?” **2. Day 7: Social Proof + Mission** - Subject: “Movement in practice” - Angle: Bring in RAW/LPP as proof this model works—link directly to page. - CTA: “Would you want a quick summary or intro call?” **3. Day 15: Event/Calendar Moment** - Subject: “Align for Green Wednesday?” - Angle: Pitch a seasonal/event/promo tie-in—timing makes the impact real and urgent, not forced. - CTA: “Would this fit with your promo calendar?” **4. Day 31: Reflection/Exit Ramp** - Subject: “Best fit on your team?” - Angle: Gentle close, ask if someone else would be better—or if they'd like to circle back. - CTA: “Is there another contact I should reach out to?” **Key Notes:** - Bring in the RAW/LPP page via natural anchor text—“see how this looks in action”, not “click here”. - For both MSOs and indies, keep CTAs soft and owner-centric; always sound collaborative. - Rotate angles for wide relevance—visual, proof, timing, exit. - No pressure or urgency triggers. --- Would you like subject lines and the exact CTA questions polished for each step before you see the full drafts? Or do you have a preference about which angle (visual/custom, peer proof, event moment, or handoff) to lean hardest into for your audience mix? 11/12/2025, 11:46:21 PM Don't worry about that at this time. finalized_strategy:
## Constraints
- always include somewhere the LPP/RAW partnership link (https://www.lastprisonerproject.org/raw-take-action) using bold, underlined embedded hyperlinks.
- Never use aggressive, pushy, or mass-market tactics; maintain collaborative, respectful tone throughout.
- Start with a nice greeting. Then skip a line after, like a real letter or email. Then say only in the first email, I found your contact on leafly. Make it sound natural. 
- Do not use “Hemp Wick” in subject lines.
- Blend storytelling and actionable next steps, ensuring each message introduces a fresh angle.
- Address both large MSOs and small shops without generic phrasing.
- Keep all emails concise, non-repetitive, and value-driven

We are NOT offering a co-brand with LLP and RAW, we are just showing social proof that RAW did a co-brand with LLP and their store can too!


1. Target Audience

Decision-makers across the cannabis retail spectrum:

• Multi-state operators (MSOs)
• Regional chains
• Independently owned dispensaries
• Founders, co-founders, buyers, category managers, and retail directors

These people live in the tension between operations and authenticity.
They want to run a smooth machine — but they also want cultural credibility, deeper customer connection, and meaningful differentiation in a crowded market.

This brief speaks directly to that pressure.

⸻

2. Campaign Purpose

The campaign activates two intertwined goals:

A. Sell Co-Branded Hemp Wick (High-Margin Retail, High Cultural Value)

• Simple to onboard
• High perceived value
• Tiny footprint
• Cultural legitimacy signal
• Perfect as a giveaway, impulse item, or in-house brand asset

B. Advance The Last Prisoner Project

Our message:
Cannabis cannot celebrate the legal industry while people remain incarcerated for the same plant.

Co-branding with LPP turns every roll distributed into a quiet act of justice:
• Legal aid
• Reentry support
• Policy advocacy
• Human dignity restored

⸻

3. Core Truth This Campaign Operates On

Hemp wick has soul.

It’s meaningless to outsiders.
It’s a lighthouse to connoisseurs.

A chain that carries it — especially custom rolls or LPP co-branded versions — sends a single, powerful cultural message:

“We understand the ritual. We respect the craft.”

This is the only product that gives chains:
• Cultural authenticity
• Ethical positioning
• Low-cost margin
• High emotional resonance

All at once.

⸻

4. Messaging Pillars (The AI Must Always Return To These)

1. Cultural Credibility

Hemp wick earns respect from people who actually shape cannabis culture—lifers, connoisseurs, ritualists.
Chains want that respect, but can’t manufacture it. Hemp wick gives it to them.

2. Ethical Alignment (LPP)

The LPP partnership turns a simple product into a meaningful gesture:

One small roll → One small act of justice.

This is how dispensaries shift from “store” to citizen of the culture.

3. Founder-to-Founder Honesty

Speak plainly.
No hype.
No “marketing tone.”

Everything should feel like one entrepreneur writing to another with a real idea worth considering.

4. Concrete Value for Retailers

• High-margin
• Zero-waste manufacturing
• European-quality wick
• Proven model (LPP co-branding partnership)
• Easy custom branding
• Perfect for giveaways and customer engagement
• Takes almost no shelf space

5. Differentiation

Every dispensary claims “quality.”
Almost none demonstrate it.

Hemp wick — especially co-branded — becomes a physical signal of care, craft, and identity.

⸻

5. Required References (Softly Embedded When Relevant)

The AI must incorporate as context, not as copy/paste:

• Sustainable European hemp + natural beeswax
• NMU = manufacturer, not a reseller
• Zero-waste facility
• Millions of lighters displaced
• Premium craft-level quality
• RAW x LPP co-branding as proof
• LPP mission: justice for people impacted by prohibition
• Green Wednesday, 4/20, product drops, or local events as timing hooks
• Custom mockup offer (always low pressure)
• No attachments — can offer to send a visual

⸻

6. Constraints (Hard Limits)

The AI must obey:

SPAM-AVOIDANCE

• No forbidden words (free, guarantee, click here, limited time, etc.)
• No sales hype, urgency, or caps
• Short subject lines (3–5 words)
• Plaintext subjects
• 3–4 short HTML paragraphs
• 1–2 links max
• No attachments
• One question at the end

STYLE

• Founder-to-founder tone
• Midwestern clarity
• Warm, concise, grounded
• No buzzwords
• Never generic
• No flattery

FOLLOW-UPS

Each follow-up must introduce a NEW angle:
Day 3 → curiosity
Day 7 → alignment + proof
Day 15 → timing
Day 31 → reflection / exit
Monthly → insight + check-in

Never reuse phrasing.
Always advance the story.

⸻

7. The “Connoisseur Credibility” Section (MANDATORY)

This must act as the emotional engine behind all emails:

Chains are strong operationally but weak culturally.
Connoisseurs do not trust them.
Hemp wick bridges that gap instantly.

A small roll signals understanding of the ritual and the culture.
It humanizes the brand.
It creates connection at almost no cost.
It elevates the store from “retail” to participant in the craft.

This angle is the campaign’s secret weapon.
It is the narrative that makes big operators stop and think.

⸻

8. The ONE Core CTA

The campaign revolves around a single action:

“Want me to show you how your logo would look on a roll?”

It’s tactile.
It’s visual.
It’s easy.
It’s commitment-free.

This is the strongest possible low-pressure conversion tool for inbound interest.

⸻

9. Required Knowledge (Internal Use Only)

The AI must understand — but not dump — that NMU:
• Manufactures its own wick in Europe
• Coats in natural beeswax
• Runs a sustainable production facility
• Has engineered zero-waste sampling
• Is the original self-dispensing roll maker
• Has worked with RAW and LPP
• Ships worldwide

It should use this knowledge sparingly, as seasoning, not as paragraphs.

No dumping.
No bragging.
Just truth.

⸻

10. Emotional Positioning

Every email should feel like this:

• Calm
• Honest
• Collaborative
• A little soulful
• No pressure
• Made by someone who builds things that matter

People respond to that.

Especially founders.
